question: A FEEL expression evaluates `string join(["a"], "X")`. How does the two-argument `string join(list, delimiter)` treat the delimiter when the list contains a single element?

options.text: `"a"` — the delimiter is only inserted between elements, so a single-element list has no delimiter.
`"aX"` — the delimiter is appended after every element.
`"Xa"` — the delimiter is prepended to every element.
`"XaX"` — the delimiter is wrapped around the joined value as both prefix and suffix.

a.text: The docs state that the resulting string contains a `delimiter` between each element. With a single element there is no "between", so the delimiter is not emitted, matching the docs example `string join(["a"], "X")` → `"a"`.

b.text: The delimiter is interposed between elements, not appended after each one. The two-argument form has no concept of a trailing delimiter.

c.text: There is no leading delimiter either; the two-argument form only inserts delimiters between consecutive elements.

d.text: Prefix/suffix wrapping is the behaviour of the four-argument overload `string join(list, delimiter, prefix, suffix)`, not the two-argument form.

explanation: Two-argument `string join(list, delimiter)` places the delimiter only between adjacent elements. A single-element list produces just that element with no delimiter, exactly like Java's `Collectors.joining(delimiter)`.
